topic: Someone's childhood home is about to be demolished
raw_output:
[story 1]

The jackhammer vibrated through the floorboards, a relentless, percussive heartbeat against the silence of the afternoon. Elias kept glancing at the window, his eyes darting to the skeletal remains of the oak tree in the yard, as if expecting movement in the shadows. Every scrape of metal against concrete sounded like a deliberate intrusion. He checked the deadbolt on the back door for the tenth time, the mechanism cold and unforgiving under his trembling fingers. *They’re coming for everything,* he thought, a knot tightening in his stomach that refused to loosen. He started stacking the few remaining framed photographs onto the mantelpiece, arranging them in a precise, defensive line. If he could just make the space look… settled, perhaps they wouldn't notice the emptiness.

[story 2]

"It's just demolition, Clara," Mr. Henderson had said, his voice booming with the confidence of a man who owned the blueprints. But when Elias looked at the heavy machinery idling at the curb, all he saw was an invasion. He traced the outline of a crack in the plaster wall, his breath catching shallowly. He’d started leaving small offerings by the front door—a polished stone, a perfectly ripe apple—hoping to appease whatever unseen force was orchestrating this destruction. The dust kicked up by the heavy trucks seemed thick, almost sentient, swirling around his ankles. He pressed his palms against his ears, trying to filter out the low, grinding sound, convinced that the noise itself was a signal, a warning broadcast directly to him.

[story 3]

I couldn't sleep. The house felt too open, too exposed, the walls seeming to breathe in and out with a shallow, unsettling rhythm. I kept running my hands over the wallpaper in the hallway, searching for seams, for anything that looked misplaced. Every shadow stretched and warped in the slivers of light filtering through the grime-streaked windows, morphing into shapes that weren't quite right—too tall, too thin. I whispered reassurances to the empty rooms, my voice cracking on the word 'safe.' When the first heavy truck rumbled past, I bolted to the attic, fumbling for a heavy lamp, needing a tangible thing to anchor me against the dizzying sense that the foundations of my entire existence were dissolving beneath my feet.
